questions.question: Which form is used in Type 0 conditionals (general truths)?
Which form is used in Type 1 conditionals (real future)?
Complete: If you heat water to 100°C, it ___.
Complete: If it rains tomorrow, we ___ at home.
Complete: If you study hard, you ___ the exam.
Which sentence is correctly Type 1?
options: If it will rain, we will stay home.
If it rains, we will stay home.
If it rained, we will stay home.
If it rains, we stayed home.

questions.question: Complete (Type 0): If plants don't get water, they ___.
Complete (Type 1): If you don't hurry, you ___ the bus.
In which conditional type can you replace 'if' with 'when' without changing the meaning?
Complete: If the sun ___, we will go to the beach.
Complete (Type 0): If you mix red and blue, you ___ purple.
Complete (Type 1): If she calls me, I ___ her back.
Which sentence is Type 0?
options: If I win, I will celebrate.
If you boil water, it evaporates.
If I had time, I would help.
If it had rained, we would have stayed.

questions.question: Which sentence is Type 1?
options: If I win, I will celebrate.
If you boil water, it evaporates.
If I had time, I would help.
If I had won, I would have celebrated.

options: If it rains, I will stay home.
If you press this button, the light turns on.

Identify the type: 'If we leave now, we will catch the train.'
Choose the correct Type 1 sentence:
options: If she will arrive, we'll start.

options: If you will press the button, the light turns on.
If you press the button, the light turns on.
If you press the button, the light will turn on.
If you pressed the button, the light turns on.

questions.question: Complete (Type 1): If the weather is good, we ___ a picnic.

Choose the right ending: 'If the lights go off, ___'
Complete: 'If you keep eating cake every day, you ___ weight.' (realistic warning)
Complete: 'If the file is too big, the email ___ through.' (general truth)
Which sentence mixes types incorrectly?
options: If I have time, I will help.
If I had time, I would help.
If you call her, she will be happy.
If you will call her, she is happy.

questions.question: Complete: 'Unless it rains, the picnic ___' (Tip: 'unless' means 'if not')
Complete: 'If you don't believe me, ___ Mark.' (imperative)
Best continuation for: 'If I win the match tomorrow, ___'
options: I will qualify for the finals.
I would qualify for the finals.
I would have qualified for the finals.

questions.question: Spot the correct sentence:
options: If you will study, you will pass.
If you study, you will pass.
If you studied, you will pass.

questions.question: Which is Type 0? 'When my dog hears the doorbell, ___'
Choose the Type 1 with 'might': 'If we leave at 6, we ___ catch the early train.'
Decide if Type 0 or Type 1: 'If you charge the battery overnight, it lasts all day.'
Decide: 'If you charge the battery tonight, you'll have power tomorrow.'
Pick the right form: 'If the train ___ on time, we'll be home by 7.'
Pick the right form: 'If I ___ time tomorrow, I'll call you back.'
Choose the most idiomatic Type 1 with continuous: 'If you ___ for me, I'll be downstairs in five minutes.'
Choose the right form for a polite request: 'If you ___ so kind, would you close the door?'
Which sentence uses Type 1 to issue a polite warning?
options: If you keep doing this, problems arise.
If you keep doing this, you will get into trouble.
If you would keep doing this, you would get into trouble.
If you had kept doing this, you would have got into trouble.

questions.question: Identify the error: 'If it will be sunny tomorrow, we will go hiking.'
options: 'If it will be' should be 'If it is'

questions.question: Explain the difference between Type 0 and Type 1 conditionals in your own words.
questions.sampleAnswer: Type 0 is for general truths or things that always happen — both clauses use the present simple (If you heat water, it boils). Type 1 is for one specific, realistic future situation — present in the if-clause, will + infinitive in the main clause (If it rains tomorrow, we will stay home).

questions.question: Build a Type 1 sentence about studying for an exam.
questions.sampleAnswer: If I study hard, I will pass the exam. (If + present simple in the if-clause, will + infinitive in the main clause.)

questions.question: Give a Type 0 example about a daily routine.
questions.sampleAnswer: If I drink coffee in the evening, I can't sleep at night. Both clauses use the present simple because the result is always the same — that's the rule for Type 0.

questions.question: Why is 'If it will rain, we will stay home' wrong? Explain.
questions.sampleAnswer: In Type 1 conditionals you never use 'will' in the if-clause — only in the main clause. The correct form is 'If it rains, we will stay home.' The if-clause takes the present simple even when the meaning is future.

questions.question: Rewrite: 'You don't water the flowers. They die.' as one Type 0 sentence.
questions.sampleAnswer: If you don't water the flowers, they die. Type 0 because this is a general rule that always holds.

questions.question: Write a Type 1 sentence using a modal other than 'will' (e.g. can, may, might).
questions.sampleAnswer: If you finish your homework, you can watch TV. ('Can' replaces 'will' in the main clause to express permission rather than a simple future fact.)

questions.question: Which form is used in Type 2 conditionals (unreal present)?
options: If + past perfect, would have + past participle

questions.question: Complete: If I ___ a million euros, I would buy a Tesla.
Complete: If I were you, I ___ apologise.
Which 'be'-form is traditionally used in Type 2 for ALL persons?
Complete: If she ___ here, she would help us.
Complete: If I knew the answer, I ___ you.
Type 2 is used for situations that are…
options: unreal or unlikely in the present

questions.question: Complete: If I were a bird, I ___ south for winter.
Complete: If she had more time, she ___ the gym.
Which sentence is a Type 2?
options: If it rains, we will stay home.
If it rained, we would stay home.
If it had rained, we would have stayed home.
If it rains, we stay home.

questions.question: Complete: If I ___ taller, I would be a basketball player.
Complete: If we lived in Spain, we ___ Spanish every day.

options: If I were rich, I would buy a yacht.
If I had wings, I would fly.
If I would have money, I would help.
If she knew, she would tell us.

questions.question: Complete: I would visit you every day if I ___ a car.
Complete (with 'could'): If I had a car, I ___ drive you to the station.
Complete: If they ___ closer, we would visit them more often.
What does 'If I were you, I would apologise' express?
Complete: If I ___ better at maths, I would help you.
Complete: She would be happier if she ___ more friends.
Type 1 vs Type 2 — which describes a realistic chance?
Complete: What ___ you do if you saw a ghost?
Complete: I ___ help you if I knew how.
Complete: If you ___ harder, you would get better grades.
Complete: If our team ___ the game, we'd be in the finals.
Choose the Type 2: 'If I had three wishes, ___'
options: I would have wished for peace.

questions.question: Complete: If he ___ a girlfriend, he wouldn't be so lonely.
Complete: If we ___ in summer, the days would be longer.
Complete: If I were the teacher, I ___ less homework.
Complete: 'Would you mind if I ___ the window?' (polite request)
Complete: If she ___ German, she would understand the lyrics.
Complete: If we ___ cheaper tickets, we would go to the concert.
Complete: If my phone ___ broken, I could call you.
options: B and C are both common

questions.question: Which describes a daydream?
options: If it rains, we'll stay home.
If I were famous, I'd live in Hollywood.
If you press this, the light comes on.

questions.question: Complete (Type 2 with 'might'): If she studied, she ___ pass.

Complete: If we ___ a swimming pool, summer would be better.
Complete: I'd be more relaxed if I ___ a holiday.
Complete: If she ___ less coffee, she would sleep better.

questions.question: Pick the most natural Type 2: 'If our school ___ a music room, we ___ band practice there.'
Choose: 'If he ___ a job, he ___ his own apartment.'

options: If I am you, I will arrive early.
If I were you, I would arrive early.
If I had been you, I would have arrived early.
If I be you, I arrive early.

questions.question: Complete (Type 2 about a hypothetical class trip): 'If the trip ___ free, more students ___ go.'
Identify the right pairing: 'I ___ your help if I ___ stuck.'
Most natural sentence?
options: If I would have to choose, I'd take this one.
If I had to choose, I'd take this one.
If I would chose, I'd take this one.
If I have to choose, I would take this one.

questions.question: Pick the right word: 'I wish I ___ ten years younger.'
Best continuation: 'If I weren't so tired, ___'
Which is hypothetical/unreal?
options: If you mix red and blue, you get purple.
If I had a million euros, I'd buy a house.
If you had told me, I would have come.

questions.question: Pick: 'It ___ easier if everyone ___ the rules.'
Complete: 'Would you help me if I ___ you?'
Type 1 vs Type 2 — best fit for: 'I'm not in Spain. I'm here.' (use Type 2)
options: If I am in Spain, I will swim.
If I were in Spain, I would swim.
If I had been in Spain, I would have swum.
If I be in Spain, I swim.

questions.question: Spot the natural sentence:
options: If I would know, I would tell you.
If I knew, I would tell you.
If I know, I tell you.
If I had known, I tell you.

questions.question: Polite-business style: 'We ___ grateful if you ___ the documents by Friday.'
Best paraphrase of 'If only I had a car!' as Type 2:
options: If I have a car, I will drive.
If I had a car, I would drive.
If I had had a car, I would have driven.
If I would have a car, I would drive.

questions.question: Explain in your own words when to use Type 2 conditionals.
questions.sampleAnswer: Type 2 is for situations in the present (or near future) that are unreal — either not true or very unlikely. The form is If + past simple, would + infinitive. Example: If I were rich, I would buy a yacht. The 'past' isn't really past — it just signals 'imaginary'.

questions.question: Build a Type 2 sentence about being a teacher.
questions.sampleAnswer: If I were a teacher, I would not give homework on weekends. Type 2 because I am not a teacher — it's hypothetical. Use 'were' for all persons in Type 2.

questions.question: Why is 'If I would be you, I would help' wrong? Correct it.
questions.sampleAnswer: In Type 2 you never use 'would' in the if-clause — only past simple (and 'were' for the verb 'be' with all persons). The correct form is: If I were you, I would help.

questions.question: Why is 'were' used instead of 'was' in 'If I were you'?
questions.sampleAnswer: In Type 2 conditionals 'were' is traditionally used for all persons (I, he, she, it) — it's a leftover of the old subjunctive in English. In speech you sometimes hear 'was', but in school and in formal writing 'were' is the correct form: If I were you, if she were here.

questions.question: Which form is Type 3?
options: If + past perfect, would have + past participle

questions.question: Complete: If I ___ harder, I would have passed.
Complete: If she had taken the train, she ___ on time.
Type 3 talks about events that…
options: did not happen in the past

Complete: If they ___ missed the goal, they would have won.

options: If she had study, she would have passed.

If she saw the sign, she wouldn't get lost.

questions.question: Complete: 'I ___ angry if you ___ apologised.'

options: we would have arrived on time.

questions.question: Spot the natural sentence:
options: If I had took the early bus, I would have been on time.

Find the wrong sentence:

questions.question: Type 3 with negation in both clauses: 'I went, so I saw the show.' becomes…
options: If I hadn't gone, I wouldn't have seen the show.
If I didn't go, I wouldn't see the show.
If I haven't gone, I haven't seen the show.
If I wouldn't have gone, I wouldn't have seen the show.

questions.question: Best transformation of 'They lost because they didn't train enough.' (Type 3):
options: If they trained enough, they wouldn't lose.
If they hadn't trained enough, they wouldn't have lost.
If they had trained enough, they wouldn't have lost.
If they would have trained enough, they wouldn't have lost.

questions.question: Explain when to use Type 3 conditionals.
questions.sampleAnswer: Type 3 is for situations in the past that did not happen — typical for regret, reproach, or 'what could have been'. Form: If + past perfect, would have + past participle. Example: If I had studied, I would have passed (= I didn't study, I didn't pass).

questions.question: Build a Type 3 sentence about missing a bus and being late.
questions.sampleAnswer: If I hadn't missed the bus, I wouldn't have been late. The reality being expressed: I missed the bus, so I was late. Type 3 always inverts the real situation.

questions.question: Why is 'If I would have known, I would have come' wrong? Correct it.
questions.sampleAnswer: In Type 3 you never use 'would have' in the if-clause — the if-clause needs the past perfect (had + past participle). Correct: If I had known, I would have come.

questions.question: What is the real situation behind 'If she had studied, she would have passed'?
questions.sampleAnswer: Type 3 always inverts reality. So this sentence implies that she did NOT study and she did NOT pass. The speaker is talking about regret or about what could have been but wasn't.

questions.question: What is a 'mixed conditional'?
options: A sentence where the if- and main-clause are in different time frames
A combination of present and future
A conditional with two different verbs

questions.question: Variant 1 (past condition → present result): which form?
options: If + past simple, would have + past participle

questions.question: Variant 2 (present condition → past result): which form?
options: If + past simple, would have + past participle

questions.question: Complete: 'If I had studied medicine, I ___ a doctor now.'
Complete: 'If I were taller, I ___ the basketball team last year.'
Complete: 'If she hadn't missed her flight, she ___ in Paris right now.'
Identify: 'If I had bought that house ten years ago, I would be rich today.' This is…
Complete: 'If he weren't so shy, he ___ to her last night.'
Complete: 'If we had taken the early train, we ___ home by now.'
Complete: 'If I knew French, I ___ that job in Lyon.'
Complete: 'If you had told me yesterday, I ___ what to do now.'
Which of these is a Mixed Conditional?
options: If it rains, I will stay home.
If I were rich, I would buy a yacht.
If I had studied harder, I would be a doctor now.
If I had studied, I would have passed.

questions.question: Complete (Variant 1): 'If she ___ the train, she ___ here already.'

Complete: 'If she were more careful, she ___ the vase yesterday.'

Complete: 'If we had left earlier, we ___ on the beach by now.'
Complete: 'If I were better at maths, I ___ the test more easily.'
Best meaning of 'If she had taken the job, she would be in London now.':
options: She took the job, she's in London.
She didn't take the job, she's not in London now.
She might take the job and be in London.
She will take the job and be in London.

questions.question: Best meaning of 'If I weren't so impatient, I wouldn't have shouted at him yesterday.':
options: I am impatient (in general), so I shouted at him yesterday.
I'm patient, so I didn't shout.
I might be impatient and shout.
If I become patient, I won't shout.

questions.question: Decide which type: 'If I had practised more, I'd be a better player today.'

options: If I had been Italian, I speak Italian.
If I were Italian, I would speak Italian.
If I had been Italian, I would speak Italian fluently now.

questions.question: Best Mixed-Conditional rephrasing of 'I didn't sleep well last night, so I'm tired now.':
options: If I sleep well, I'm not tired.
If I had slept well last night, I wouldn't be tired now.
If I had slept well, I wouldn't have been tired.
If I would sleep well, I wouldn't be tired.

questions.question: Best Mixed-Conditional rephrasing of 'I'm a fast reader, that's why I finished the book in one day.':
options: If I am a fast reader, I finish books quickly.
If I were a fast reader, I would finish books quickly.
If I weren't a fast reader, I wouldn't have finished the book in one day.

questions.question: Pick the most natural Mixed: 'You ___ better grades now if you ___ in Year 8.'
options: would have got / had paid attention

questions.question: Most natural: 'If we ___ that house when prices were low, we ___ a fortune today.'
Explain in your own words what a Mixed Conditional is.
questions.sampleAnswer: A Mixed Conditional is a conditional sentence where the if-clause and the main clause are in different time frames. For example, an unreal past condition leads to a present result (If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now), or a present characteristic explains a past event (If I weren't so shy, I would have asked her out).

questions.question: Build a Mixed Conditional that explains your current situation through a past decision.
questions.sampleAnswer: Example: If I had started learning Spanish three years ago, I would speak it fluently now. Past condition (had + past participle) explains current result (would + infinitive).

questions.question: What's the difference between 'If I had studied medicine, I would have been a doctor' (Type 3) and 'If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now' (Mixed)?
questions.sampleAnswer: Type 3 puts both condition and result in the past — meaning that being a doctor would have happened in the past (e.g. for a few years). The Mixed Conditional puts the result in the present — meaning that today, right now, the speaker would be a doctor. The Mixed version says the consequences are still felt today.

questions.question: Rewrite as a Mixed Conditional: 'I am tired today because I went to bed late last night.'
questions.sampleAnswer: If I hadn't gone to bed late last night, I wouldn't be tired today. Mixed Variant 1: past condition (had + past participle) → present result (would + infinitive). The past action explains the current state.
